subject: Mathematics
contextNote: Sec 2 Normal Math, 22 students. You modelled a word problem: 'Twice Ali's age plus 4 equals Sam's age. Sam is 20. Find Ali's age.' You showed how to set up 2x + 4 = 20 and solved to get x = 8. Students are now 10 minutes into three similar practice problems. The room is silent — too silent.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: All 3 correct in 6 minutes. Answers only, no working. Sitting quietly, staring ahead.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Q1 and Q2 correct. Q3 has the equation written but not solved. Not asking for help.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Q1 correct after 8 minutes. Q2 attempted but equation setup is wrong. Head down, not looking up.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Page blank except for question numbers copied. Looking around the room, not writing.

ep1.situation: 22 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the silence is striking — not the productive hum of focused work, but the flat quiet of compliance without engagement. Rajan finished all three questions in 6 minutes. His answers are correct: Ali = 8, x = 12, y = 15. But his page shows only the final answers with no working, no equations, no justification. He sits with his arms folded, staring at the front of the room, waiting. Nur Atiqah has Q1 and Q2 correct with minimal working shown. For Q3, she has written the equation 3x - 5 = 10 but has not solved it. When you pause near her desk, she does not look up or ask a question. Wei Jie is still on Q2. His Q1 is correct, but for Q2 he has written x + 10 = 25 when the correct setup should be 2x + 10 = 25. His head is down and he is erasing something. Siti's page is blank except for the question numbers 1, 2, 3 copied from the board. She is looking around the room, occasionally glancing at her neighbor's page, but not writing. A quick scan of the class: 6 students have finished all 3 questions and are sitting silently. 10 students are working slowly with minimal or no working shown. 6 students, including Siti, have barely started and are not asking for help. The room feels stuck — students are compliant but not reasoning aloud, not asking questions, not engaging. You have about 18 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: The silence reveals a participation gap, not a readiness gap. Most students can do the work — the answers suggest procedural competence — but no one is talking, questioning, or justifying. The room is compliant but cognitively passive. You cannot tell whether the silence masks understanding or confusion.

choices.label: Thumbometer + Two-Step MCQ
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and ask for a quick confidence check using thumbs: thumb up if you feel confident about your equation setups, thumb sideways if you are unsure, thumb down if you are stuck. Then project a diagnostic question on the board: 'Maya has $50. She spends $x on lunch and $12 on transport. She has $30 left. Which equation is correct? (A) x + 12 = 30  (B) 50 - x - 12 = 30  (C) x + 12 = 50.' Then ask: 'Why did you choose that answer? (1) Because I balanced both sides  (2) Because I guessed  (3) Because I worked backwards.' Ask students to write both answers on mini whiteboards and hold them up simultaneously. Use the pattern of responses to see who understands equation setup and who is just pattern-matching.
choices.tradeoff: The thumbometer gives you a quick, low-stakes gauge of confidence without requiring students to speak. The two-step MCQ reveals whether students can set up equations and explain why. It is fast (3-4 minutes) and gives you whole-class evidence. But the thumbometer may not be honest — quiet students may put thumbs sideways even if they understand, to avoid standing out. The MCQ interrupts the few students who are working productively.

choices.label: Paired think-aloud with ZPD prompts
choices.elaboration: Assign shoulder partners and ask them to work in pairs. Give the instruction: 'Explain your equation setup to your partner. Your partner listens first, then asks one question: Why did you set it up that way?' Distribute prompt cards to students who are stalled: 'Start by identifying: What do you know? What are you finding? What operation connects them?' Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one or two pairs to share their explanation with the class.
choices.tradeoff: Paired work breaks the silence in a low-risk way (Vygotsky: ZPD scaffolding through peer support). Students can talk quietly without the pressure of whole-class participation. The prompt cards support stalled students respectfully. But pair quality is uneven — if both students are stuck or both are silent, the pair will not be productive. Siti may nod along without genuinely engaging. You lose control over the accuracy of explanations being shared.

choices.label: Accountable Talk sentence stems
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and introduce Accountable Talk stems. Write on the board: 'I claim the equation is ___ because ___. The evidence for my claim is ___.' Ask students to turn to their shoulder partner and use the stem to explain their Q1 setup. The listener must respond using: 'I agree because ___' or 'I disagree because ___.' Circulate and listen for the most common reasoning patterns and errors. After 3 minutes, select two contrasting pairs — one with clear reasoning, one with an error or shallow claim — and run a brief IRF probe with the whole class: 'What did you notice about these two explanations? Which reasoning is stronger? Why?'
choices.tradeoff: Accountable Talk stems force students to make claims and justify them, not just share answers. The structure ensures reasoning is made explicit. The IRF probe allows you to surface and correct errors publicly. But the stems may feel artificial or intimidating to quiet students. Siti may mumble the stem without genuine reasoning. Some students may say 'I agree' without actually evaluating their partner's claim. The move requires students to speak, which may overwhelm the quietest students.

A.consequence: The thumbs go up and the pattern is revealing. 6 students show thumbs up (confident), 14 show thumbs sideways (unsure), and 2 show thumbs down (stuck), including Siti. The thumbometer confirms what you suspected — most students are unsure, not confident. Then the whiteboards go up for the MCQ. 16 of 22 students chose (B) 50 - x - 12 = 30, which is correct. But on the reasoning question, only 8 of those 16 chose 'I balanced both sides' — the other 8 chose 'I worked backwards' or 'I guessed.' Rajan got both questions right and when you ask him to explain, he says quietly: 'Start with 50, subtract what she spent, equals what is left.' His reasoning is correct but minimal. Nur got the equation right but chose 'I worked backwards' for the reasoning — she can execute but cannot articulate the balancing principle. Wei Jie chose (A) x + 12 = 30, which is incorrect — he is only accounting for part of the problem. Siti chose (C) x + 12 = 50, also incorrect. The evidence is now clearer: most students can set up equations procedurally, but many cannot explain why their setup is correct. The silence was masking shallow reasoning. You have 16 minutes remaining.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) feedback on unsure thumbs
choices.elaboration: Use the thumbometer evidence to form pairs: students who showed thumbs up (like Rajan) are paired with students who showed thumbs sideways (like Nur). Ask the confident student to give AQS feedback on their partner's MCQ response: Affirm what is correct ('You chose the right equation'), Question the reasoning ('Why did you say you worked backwards instead of balancing?'), Suggest how to think about it ('Try thinking: what you start with minus what you spend equals what you have left. That is balancing.'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS feedback with the class.
choices.tradeoff: AQS feedback gives quiet students structured practice in talking about their reasoning. The Affirm-Question-Suggest structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. Pairing confident with unsure students leverages the expertise in the room. But the quality of peer explanation varies. Rajan may give minimal explanations because he is not used to articulating his reasoning. If Siti is paired with a student who cannot explain well, she may copy without understanding. The confident students may disengage if they feel the task is not challenging them.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) chains on justifications
choices.elaboration: Select 3-4 students who got both MCQ questions right and can explain their reasoning. Run an IRF chain with the whole class: 'Why is equation (B) correct? What is the reasoning?' Take the first student's response (Initiation). Then ask a second student: 'Can you add to what [first student] said?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'Do you agree? Is there anything you would clarify or correct?' (Feedback). Build a complete, class-constructed explanation. Write the final reasoning on the board and ask all students to copy it into their notes.
choices.tradeoff: IRF chains (common in Singapore classrooms) build a shared explanation that is richer than any single student would produce. It makes reasoning visible and public. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident and can articulate their thinking. The majority of quiet students remain passive observers. Siti and Wei Jie, who got the MCQ wrong, have nothing to contribute and may feel exposed. The reasoning belongs to the few who speak, not to everyone.

B.consequence: The pairs begin working and the room shifts from silence to low murmurs. Rajan explains his Q1 setup to his partner in a quiet voice: '2x + 4 = 20 because twice Ali's age is 2x, plus 4 gives Sam's age, which is 20.' His partner nods and writes it down. Nur is paired with another student who also got Q1 and Q2 correct. They whisper-check each other's equations and both nod — 'Correct, correct.' No deeper reasoning emerges. Wei Jie is paired with a student who is also stuck on Q2. They both stare at the question. Wei Jie's partner says quietly: 'I think it is x + 10 = 25?' Wei Jie nods and writes it down, but neither student questions whether this is correct. The error is being reinforced. Siti is paired with a student who has Q1 correct. Her partner tries to explain: 'You need to write the equation first. What do you know?' Siti says 'I don't know' and looks down. Her partner hesitates, then says 'Just write 2x + 4 = 20' and points at the board. Siti copies it but does not explain why. You circulate and hear the pattern: 4 pairs are reasoning aloud with clear explanations. 10 pairs are procedurally checking answers ('Correct, correct') without justifying. 4 pairs are stuck in near-silence, with minimal or incorrect explanations being shared. The prompt cards help some students start, but the reasoning quality is thin. The room has more talk than before, but much of it is surface-level. 17 minutes remain.

choices.label: Tomlinson flex share: reasoning pairs teach procedural pairs
choices.elaboration: Identify the 4 pairs who were reasoning aloud clearly. Regroup the class: each reasoning pair joins with one procedural pair to form a group of 4. Ask the reasoning pair to explain their equation setup and justification to the procedural pair. The procedural pair must ask at least one clarifying question: 'Why did you set it up that way?' or 'How do you know that is correct?' Circulate and listen for whether the explanations deepen the procedural pairs' understanding.
choices.tradeoff: Flex grouping (Tomlinson) extends the ZPD scaffolding by pairing students who can reason with students who are still procedural. It gives the reasoning pairs a cognitively demanding task — teaching is harder than doing. But the procedural pairs may dominate the group by asking surface questions or just copying the reasoning without understanding it. Siti, who is still stalled, may be overwhelmed in a group of 4 and remain passive.

choices.label: Solo written justification: I know, I check
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair work and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'Choose one of your equations. Write: (1) I set up this equation because ___. (2) I know it is correct because ___. (3) I check my work by ___.' Give students 3 minutes to write, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who has genuine understanding from the pair work and who was just borrowing their partner's reasoning.
choices.tradeoff: Moving from pair talk to solo writing gives you individual evidence of understanding (Hattie & Timperley: move toward self-regulation). It reveals whether the pair work built genuine learning or just social agreement. But it kills the emerging talk momentum in the room. Students who were starting to reason aloud in pairs may resent being asked to write individually. Siti, who did not engage during pair work, will likely produce minimal or no writing.

C.consequence: The Accountable Talk stems go up on the board and pairs begin using them. The room gets louder — not noisy, but noticeably less silent than before. Rajan uses the stem with his partner: 'I claim the equation is 2x + 4 = 20 because twice Ali's age is 2x, and adding 4 gives Sam's age, which is 20. The evidence is that Sam is 20 and the problem says twice Ali's age plus 4.' His partner responds: 'I agree because that matches the problem.' The exchange is correct but feels scripted. Nur is paired with another student who also got Q1 correct. They use the stems: 'I claim 2x + 4 = 20 because it works.' 'I agree because it works.' The stems are being used, but the reasoning is shallow — they are not explaining why the equation is correct, just that it produces the right answer. Wei Jie is paired with a student who has the same error on Q2. They use the stems: 'I claim x + 10 = 25 because...' Wei Jie's partner pauses, then says 'because that is what I wrote.' Neither student questions the setup. The error is being reinforced through Accountable Talk. Siti is paired with a student who tries to use the stems: 'I claim the equation is 2x + 4 = 20 because...' Siti mumbles 'I agree' but does not offer her own claim. You circulate and hear the pattern: 4 pairs are using the stems to make genuine claims with evidence. 8 pairs are using the stems but staying shallow ('because it works', 'because I agree'). 6 pairs are stuck in near-silence or reinforcing errors. The stems have activated some talk, but much of it is compliance without depth. 16 minutes remain.

choices.label: Gallery critique with AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) on stems
choices.elaboration: Select 4 pairs' Accountable Talk stems and post them anonymously on the board: Pair A (clear reasoning with evidence), Pair B (shallow 'because it works'), Pair C (error reinforced), Pair D (silence/minimal). Ask the class to vote on which pair's reasoning is strongest and which needs the most improvement. Then run a whole-class AQS session on the weakest pair: Affirm what they attempted ('They used the stem structure'), Question what is missing ('Why is "because it works" not enough? What evidence is missing?'), Suggest how to improve ('Explain how the equation matches the problem: what each term represents and why the operations are correct'). Take responses from multiple students and build a complete AQS critique together.
choices.tradeoff: The gallery critique makes the quality of reasoning visible and public. The AQS structure models what good critique looks like. It turns the Accountable Talk output into a learning opportunity for the whole class. But voting on the 'weakest' reasoning may embarrass the students whose stems were posted, even anonymously. In a class of 22, students often recognize their own work. The move also shifts back to teacher-led instruction, which may feel like a step backward after the pair work.

choices.label: Whole-class reteach: task-level feedback on equation setup
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair work and address the shallow reasoning directly with the whole class. Write a correct equation setup on the board: '2x + 4 = 20.' Explain step by step: 'This equation is correct because: (1) Twice Ali's age is 2x. (2) Plus 4 gives 2x + 4. (3) This equals Sam's age, which is 20. So 2x + 4 = 20. Each term in the equation matches a part of the problem.' Emphasize: 'Your equation setup is correct when every term represents something from the problem and the operations match the relationships described.' Then ask all students to re-examine their Q2 and Q3 setups and write one sentence explaining why their equation is correct.
choices.tradeoff: This is task-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) — you are explaining what a correct equation setup looks like and why. It is efficient and will reach the 14 students who were unsure or shallow in their reasoning. But it shifts the room back to silence and teacher-led instruction. The students who were starting to reason aloud in pairs lose that momentum. Rajan, who already understands, is under-challenged. Siti may copy the board explanation without building genuine understanding.

A1.outcome: You pair Rajan with Nur and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Nur's MCQ reasoning. Rajan looks at Nur's whiteboard and begins quietly: 'You got the equation right — (B) is correct. But you said you worked backwards instead of balancing. Try thinking about it this way: you start with $50, subtract what you spend, and that equals what you have left. That is balancing both sides.' Nur listens, nods, and writes 'balancing' in her notes. She says softly: 'Oh, I see. It is like the problem is already balanced.' Rajan nods. Wei Jie is paired with a student who got both MCQ questions right. His partner gives AQS feedback: 'Your equation is wrong. You only counted part of the spending. Try adding both amounts she spent: x + 12. Then subtract from 50.' Wei Jie rewrites his equation as 50 - x - 12 = 30 and solves to get x = 8. He looks relieved. Siti is paired with a student who got the equation right but cannot explain the reasoning clearly. Her partner says: 'You need to write 50 minus the spending equals 30. Just write it.' Siti copies 50 - x - 12 = 30 onto her page but when you ask her 'Why is this correct?', she says 'Because my partner said so.' The room is engaged and several students are making gains through the structured feedback. But the talk is still quiet — students are whispering, not speaking at full volume. Rajan is not stretched by the task. Siti is still copying without understanding. On a follow-up check, 14 of 18 unsure students can now explain their reasoning, up from 8. The AQS feedback has activated more talk and improved reasoning for the middle group. But the quietest students are still passive, and the confident students are under-challenged. 15 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: AQS feedback gives quiet students structured practice in articulating reasoning. The Affirm-Question-Suggest structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. Nur and Wei Jie, who were in the unsure group, made clear gains through peer support. But the quality of peer explanation varies. Rajan can explain clearly but is not challenged by the task. Siti's partner cannot explain well, so Siti remains dependent on copying. The room is still quieter than ideal — students are talking, but in whispers, not with confidence.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose AQS peer feedback over whole-class discussion? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie made gains, but Siti is still copying and Rajan is under-challenged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback in quiet classes?

How do you build a classroom culture where students speak with confidence, not just compliance?

A2.outcome: You select Rajan and two other students who got both MCQ questions right and can explain their reasoning. You ask the whole class: 'Why is equation (B) 50 - x - 12 = 30 correct? What is the reasoning?' Rajan responds first (Initiation): 'Because you start with 50, subtract what Maya spent on lunch and transport, and that equals what she has left, which is 30.' You nod and ask a second student to build on this (Response): 'So the equation matches the problem. The 50 is what she started with, the x and 12 are what she spent, and 30 is what is left.' A third student evaluates (Feedback): 'Yes, and you can check by solving: 50 - x - 12 = 30, so 38 - x = 30, so x = 8. If you substitute back, 50 - 8 - 12 = 30, which is correct.' The IRF chain produces a complete, detailed explanation. You write the final reasoning on the board and ask all students to copy it into their notes. Nur listens carefully and writes the explanation down. She looks like she is processing it. Wei Jie copies the board notes but his expression is uncertain — he is following the logic but may not fully understand. Siti copies the board notes mechanically. When you ask her 'Does this make sense?', she nods quickly but does not speak. The IRF chain has made the reasoning visible and public, but it is driven by the 3 students who spoke. The other 19 students are passive observers. Rajan is engaged because he was selected to speak, but he is not challenged. Nur and Wei Jie are listening, but you cannot tell whether they genuinely understand or are just copying. Siti remains silent and dependent. The reasoning belongs to the few who spoke, not to everyone. 15 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: The IRF chain (common in Singapore classrooms) builds a shared, class-constructed explanation that is richer and more detailed than any single student would produce. It makes reasoning visible and models what good justification looks like. Nur and Wei Jie, who were unsure, heard a clear explanation. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident and articulate. The majority of quiet students remain passive observers. Siti and Wei Jie, who got the MCQ wrong, have nothing to contribute and may feel exposed. The reasoning belongs to Rajan and the few who speak, not to everyone.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose IRF chains over peer feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The reasoning is now visible, but only 3 students spoke. What does this tell you about who does the thinking in an IRF chain?

How do you design whole-class discussion so that quiet students participate, not just observe?

B1.outcome: You regroup the class into groups of 4: each reasoning pair joins with one procedural pair. You ask the reasoning pairs to explain their equation setup and justification to the procedural pairs. The procedural pairs must ask at least one clarifying question. Rajan's group begins. Rajan explains: '2x + 4 = 20 because twice Ali's age is 2x, plus 4 gives Sam's age, which is 20. Each part of the equation matches the problem.' The procedural pair asks: 'How do you know to use 2x and not just x?' Rajan responds: 'Because the problem says twice Ali's age, so you multiply by 2.' The procedural pair nods and writes this down. Nur's group works similarly — the reasoning pair explains, the procedural pair asks a clarifying question, and the explanation deepens. Wei Jie is in a group where the reasoning pair explains clearly, but Wei Jie's procedural pair dominates by asking surface questions: 'Is this right? Is that right?' without engaging with the reasoning. Siti is in a group of 4 but remains silent. When the reasoning pair explains, Siti nods but does not ask a question. When prompted by you, she says quietly: 'I don't know what to ask.' The group continues without her. You circulate and hear the pattern: 3 groups are reasoning deeply, with the procedural pairs asking genuine clarifying questions. 4 groups are staying procedural, with the procedural pairs dominating and asking surface questions. 2 groups are uneven, with one or two students remaining passive. The flex grouping has extended the talk for some students, but the procedural pairs are not always engaging deeply. Siti remains passive. 16 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: Flex grouping (Tomlinson) extends the ZPD scaffolding by pairing students who can reason with students who are still procedural. It gives the reasoning pairs a cognitively demanding task — teaching is harder than doing. Some procedural pairs asked genuine clarifying questions and deepened their understanding. But the procedural pairs often dominated the groups by asking surface questions or just copying the reasoning without engaging. Siti, who is still stalled, was overwhelmed in a group of 4 and remained passive. The groups are uneven in quality.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose flex grouping over solo work? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Some groups reasoned deeply, but others stayed procedural and Siti remained passive. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive group work in quiet classes?

How do you ensure that all students in a group are actively reasoning, not just observing or copying?

B2.outcome: You stop the pair work and ask all students to work individually. You write the prompt on the board: 'Choose one of your equations. Write: (1) I set up this equation because ___. (2) I know it is correct because ___. (3) I check my work by ___.' The room goes silent again. Rajan writes quickly: 'I set up 2x + 4 = 20 because twice Ali's age (2x) plus 4 equals Sam's age (20). I know it is correct because each term matches the problem and solving gives x = 8, which makes sense. I check by substituting: 2(8) + 4 = 20 ✓.' His response is detailed, precise, and shows metacognitive awareness. Nur writes: 'I set up 2x + 4 = 20 because the problem says twice Ali's age plus 4 equals Sam's age. I know it is correct because I solved it and got Ali = 8. I check by... I am not sure.' Her response shows she can set up equations but cannot articulate how to verify beyond solving. Wei Jie writes: 'I set up the equation because that is what the problem said. I know it is correct because I got an answer. I check by looking at it again.' His response is procedurally compliant but shows no genuine reasoning — he is describing what he did, not why it works. Siti writes one sentence: 'I set up the equation because my partner told me.' Then she stops. Her response reveals complete dependence. You collect the responses and scan them. The solo writing has given you honest individual evidence, but it has also revealed that the pair work did not build deep understanding for most students. The talk was social, not cognitive. The room is silent again, and the emerging momentum from the pair work is gone. 15 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: Solo written justification gives you clean, individual evidence of understanding, uncontaminated by pair talk (Hattie & Timperley: move toward self-regulation). Rajan's response shows deep understanding. But Nur, Wei Jie, and Siti's responses reveal that the pair work did not reach them. Nur stayed procedural, Wei Jie stayed shallow, and Siti remained completely dependent. The move to solo work killed the emerging talk momentum in the room. Students who were starting to reason aloud in pairs may feel the solo task is punitive. The evidence is honest, but the cost is high.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from pair talk to solo writing? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan shows deep understanding, but most students stayed shallow or dependent. What does this tell you about when pair talk builds real learning versus borrowed understanding?

What would need to be different about the pair task for the solo transfer to go better?

C1.outcome: You post 4 pairs' Accountable Talk stems on the board anonymously: Pair A (clear reasoning with evidence), Pair B (shallow 'because it works'), Pair C (error reinforced: x + 10 = 25), Pair D (minimal/silence). You ask the class to vote on which pair's reasoning is strongest and which needs the most improvement. Most students vote for Pair A as strongest and Pair C as needing improvement. You run a whole-class AQS session on Pair C. Several students affirm: 'They tried to use the stem structure.' You write this on the board. Then you ask: 'What should we question?' Nur raises her hand tentatively and says: 'The equation is wrong. It should be 2x + 10 = 25, not x + 10 = 25.' You write: 'Question: Why is the equation setup incorrect?' Then you ask: 'What would you suggest?' Another student says: 'Check the problem again. It says twice the number, so you need 2x, not just x.' You write: 'Suggest: Read the problem carefully. Twice the number means 2x.' The AQS feedback is now complete and visible on the board. You ask all students to re-examine their Q2 and Q3 setups using the AQS structure. Nur re-checks her Q3 and realizes she has not solved it yet. She solves 3x - 5 = 10 and gets x = 5. Wei Jie looks at the board, erases his Q2 equation, and rewrites it as 2x + 10 = 25. He solves and gets x = 7.5. He looks pleased. Rajan was already correct and does not need to re-check, but he listens to the AQS process with mild interest. Siti copies the AQS feedback from the board into her notes but when you ask her to re-check her Q1 setup, she hesitates and does not write anything. The student whose Pair C stems were critiqued looks uncomfortable — you notice them glancing around to see if anyone recognizes their work. The AQS process has turned the Accountable Talk output into a structured learning moment, but it has also created a moment of public exposure. The room is quieter now than during the pair work. 15 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS feedback structure is concrete and actionable because it is based on real student work from the Accountable Talk pairs. It models what good critique looks like and makes the error correction process transparent. Nur and Wei Jie, who were in the middle group, made clear gains by seeing the error and the correction. But the student whose work was critiqued feels exposed, even with anonymous posting. In a class of 22, students often recognize their own work. Siti is still copying rather than reasoning — the AQS process did not move her from dependence to understanding. The move also shifts back to teacher-led instruction, which kills the talk momentum from the pair work.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to make the error public and use AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn from this?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie self-corrected, but one student felt exposed and Siti is still copying. What does this tell you about using student work publicly in quiet classes?

How do you build a classroom culture where errors are learning opportunities rather than sources of embarrassment?

C2.outcome: You stop the Accountable Talk pair work and address the shallow reasoning directly with the whole class. You write a correct equation setup on the board: '2x + 4 = 20.' You explain step by step: 'This equation is correct because: (1) Twice Ali's age is 2x. (2) Plus 4 gives 2x + 4. (3) This equals Sam's age, which is 20. So 2x + 4 = 20. Each term in the equation matches a part of the problem. Your equation setup is correct when every term represents something from the problem and the operations match the relationships described.' The room goes quiet as students watch and copy. Nur writes the explanation into her notes and then looks at her Q3. She realizes she has the equation 3x - 5 = 10 but has not solved it. She solves it now and gets x = 5. Wei Jie looks at the board explanation and compares it to his Q2 equation. He sees his error — he wrote x + 10 = 25 instead of 2x + 10 = 25. He erases and rewrites the equation correctly, then solves to get x = 7.5. He looks relieved. Rajan listens to the reteach with visible disengagement. He already understands this and the whole-class explanation does not challenge him. He returns to staring at the front of the room, waiting. Siti copies the board explanation line by line into her notes. When you circulate after the reteach and ask her to attempt Q1 using the explanation, she hesitates and does not write anything. On a follow-up check, 18 of 22 students can now set up equations correctly, up from 16. The shallow reasoning has been addressed for most of the class through direct task-level feedback. But the room is silent again — the talk momentum from the Accountable Talk pairs is gone. Rajan is under-challenged and disengaged. Siti is still dependent on copying rather than understanding. The whole-class reteach served the middle group but missed the extremes and killed the emerging participation. 15 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with task-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) is efficient and reaches the majority of students who were reasoning shallowly. It explains what a correct equation setup looks like and why. Nur and Wei Jie, who were in the middle group, made clear gains. But the move treats the class as uniform and shifts back to teacher-led instruction. Rajan, who already understood, is under-challenged and disengaged. Siti, who is still at the access stage, remains passive and dependent on copying. The room is silent again — the Accountable Talk momentum is gone. The whole-class approach served the middle well but missed the students at the top and bottom and killed the emerging participation.
C2.reflection:
Why did you choose whole-class reteach over continuing the pair work? What were you prioritizing — efficiency, clarity, or participation?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Most students can now set up equations, but the room is silent again and Siti is still copying. What does this tell you about the trade-off between direct instruction and student talk in quiet classes?

How do you balance the need for clear explanations with the need to activate student voice?
